content.text: Spot Pricing

Azure Spot VMs give you access to unused Azure compute capacity at discounts of up to 90% compared to pay-as-you-go rates. The tradeoff is that Azure can evict your VM with 30 seconds notice when the capacity is needed for standard-priority workloads or when the current spot price exceeds your maximum price cap.

Spot pricing is ideal for workloads that can tolerate interruptions: batch data processing, CI/CD build agents, machine learning training jobs, video rendering, and stateless web workers. It is not suitable for stateful services, databases, or applications that cannot checkpoint and resume.

attrs.alt: Azure Spot VM pricing and eviction flow diagram

content.text: How Spot Pricing Works

Unlike AWS spot instances where you bid in a classic auction, Azure spot pricing uses a capacity-availability model. The spot price for a VM SKU in a region fluctuates based on current unused capacity. You set a maximum price you are willing to pay; if the spot price rises above that cap or Azure needs the capacity back, your VM is evicted.
Concept
Detail
Spot price
Current $/hour for the SKU in that region — changes dynamically
Max price
Your ceiling. -1 means 'never evict due to price' (only for capacity reclaim)
Eviction type
Deallocate (keep disks/NIC) or Delete (remove everything)
Eviction notice
30-second Scheduled Event via IMDS before the VM is stopped
Capacity eviction
Azure reclaims capacity for standard workloads regardless of your max price
Discount range
Up to 90% vs PAYG, typically 60-80% for common SKUs

Setting max price to -1 means you accept the current spot price up to the PAYG rate and will only be evicted when Azure genuinely needs capacity — you will never be evicted purely for a price spike above your cap.
Creating Spot VMs with the CLI

# Create a single Spot VM (Deallocate on eviction, max price = PAYG rate)
az vm create \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name spot-worker-01 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --priority Spot \
  --eviction-policy Deallocate \
  --max-price -1 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Create a Spot VM with an explicit max price cap ($0.15/hr)
# The VM will be evicted if spot price exceeds $0.15/hr
az vm create \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name spot-worker-capped \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --priority Spot \
  --eviction-policy Deallocate \
  --max-price 0.15 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Check current spot price history for a SKU
az vm list-skus --location eastus --size Standard_D4s_v5 --output table

# Check spot eviction rate (low/medium/high) — shown in Portal under VM size selection
# CLI: no direct eviction rate command; use Portal or Pricing API
Spot VM Scale Sets: The Production Pattern

Running a single Spot VM in production is risky — a single eviction stops your workload. The production pattern is a VM Scale Set (VMSS) with a mixed-instance policy: some on-demand instances as a baseline and the rest as spot, spread across availability zones. VMSS automatically replaces evicted spot nodes.

# Create a VMSS with mixed on-demand + spot instances
az vmss create \
  --resource-group <rg-name> \
  --name spot-worker-vmss \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --vm-sku Standard_D4s_v5 \
  --instance-count 5 \
  --priority Spot \
  --eviction-policy Delete \
  --max-price -1 \
  --single-placement-group false \
  --zones 1 2 3 \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# For mixed on-demand + spot (requires JSON template or ARM/Bicep):
# Set orchestrationMode=Flexible and define multiple SKU tiers with capacities

Using multiple VM SKUs in the skuProfile increases the chance of finding available capacity. Azure tries each SKU in order of lowest price. Spreading across 3 availability zones further reduces the chance of simultaneous eviction.
Handling Eviction Gracefully with Scheduled Events

Azure sends a Scheduled Event 30 seconds before evicting a Spot VM. Your application should poll the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint on the VM and initiate a graceful shutdown — flushing buffers, checkpointing state, draining connections — within that window.
Spot Pricing with Azure Batch

Azure Batch natively supports spot (low-priority) nodes in a pool. When a low-priority node is evicted, Batch automatically requeues the interrupted tasks. This makes Batch the simplest option for embarrassingly parallel batch workloads on spot capacity.

import azure.batch as batch
import azure.batch.models as batchmodels
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.batch import BatchServiceClient

# Authenticate
credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
batch_client = BatchServiceClient(
    credential,
    batch_url="https://<account>.eastus.batch.azure.com"
)

# Create a pool with low-priority (spot) nodes
pool_config = batchmodels.PoolAddParameter(
    id="spot-pool",
    vm_size="Standard_D4s_v5",
    target_dedicated_nodes=0,          # no on-demand
    target_low_priority_nodes=10,      # 10 spot nodes
    virtual_machine_configuration=batchmodels.VirtualMachineConfiguration(
        image_reference=batchmodels.ImageReference(
            publisher="Canonical",
            offer="0001-com-ubuntu-server-jammy",
            sku="22_04-lts",
            version="latest"
        ),
        node_agent_sku_id="batch.node.ubuntu 22.04",
    ),
)

batch_client.pool.add(pool_config)
print("Spot pool created. Interrupted tasks will be automatically requeued.")
Checking Current Spot Prices
Before choosing a region or SKU, check the current spot price and historical eviction rates. Azure exposes a Retail Prices API that includes spot pricing.

import requests

def get_spot_prices(sku: str = "Standard_D4s_v5", region: str = "eastus"):
    """Fetch current spot prices for a given SKU from the Azure Retail Prices API."""
    url = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
    params = {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": (
            f"serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' "
            f"and skuName eq '{sku} Spot' "
            f"and armRegionName eq '{region}'"
        ),
    }
    resp = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=10)
    items = resp.json().get("Items", [])
    if not items:
        print(f"No spot price found for {sku} in {region}.")
        return

    for item in items[:3]:
        print(f"SKU: {item['skuName']}")
        print(f"  Region: {item['armRegionName']}")
        print(f"  OS: {item.get('productName', '')}")
        print(f"  Spot price: ${item['retailPrice']:.4f}/hr")
        print(f"  PAYG price: ${item.get('unitPrice', 0):.4f}/hr")
        savings = (1 - item['retailPrice'] / item['unitPrice']) * 100 if item.get('unitPrice') else 0
        print(f"  Savings: {savings:.1f}%")

get_spot_prices("Standard D4s v5", "eastus")
Spot VMs for CI/CD Build Agents

A common production use case is running GitHub Actions self-hosted runners or Azure DevOps agents on spot VMs. Because each job runs for a bounded time (rarely more than 30 minutes), the probability of eviction mid-job is low, and savings are substantial for large build fleets.

# Startup script to register a GitHub Actions runner on a Spot VM
# Embed in VM custom data or run via az vm extension
#!/bin/bash
set -e
RUNNER_VERSION="2.317.0"
RUNNER_TOKEN="<runner-registration-token>"
GITHUB_ORG="<org-name>"
RUNNER_NAME="spot-$(hostname)"

cd /home/azureuser
mkdir -p actions-runner && cd actions-runner

# Download runner
curl -sL -o runner.tar.gz \
  "https://github.com/actions/runner/releases/download/v${RUNNER_VERSION}/actions-runner-linux-x64-${RUNNER_VERSION}.tar.gz"
tar xzf runner.tar.gz

# Configure and start
./config.sh \
  --url "https://github.com/${GITHUB_ORG}" \
  --token "${RUNNER_TOKEN}" \
  --name "${RUNNER_NAME}" \
  --labels "spot,linux,x64" \
  --unattended

./svc.sh install
./svc.sh start

# VMSS-based self-hosted runners: scale automatically with jobs
# Scale out: add spot nodes when queue depth increases
az monitor autoscale create \
  --resource-group <rg> \
  --resource /subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets/spot-runner-vmss \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachineScaleSets \
  --name autoscale-runners \
  --min-count 2 \
  --max-count 20 \
  --count 2

az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --autoscale-name autoscale-runners \
  --resource-group <rg> \
  --scale out 3 \
  --condition "Percentage CPU > 70 avg 5m"

az monitor autoscale rule create \
  --autoscale-name autoscale-runners \
  --resource-group <rg> \
  --scale in 2 \
  --condition "Percentage CPU < 20 avg 10m"
Spot VMs for Machine Learning Training

Long-running ML training jobs need checkpointing to survive eviction. The standard pattern is to save model state to Azure Blob Storage every N steps. On restart (either after manual requeue or VMSS replacement), the training script loads the latest checkpoint and resumes.

import torch, os
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient

ACCOUNT_URL = "https://<storage-account>.blob.core.windows.net"
CONTAINER = "checkpoints"
JOB_ID = os.environ.get("JOB_ID", "default")

blob_client = BlobServiceClient(account_url=ACCOUNT_URL)

def save_checkpoint(model, optimizer, epoch: int, step: int):
    path = f"/tmp/ckpt-{epoch}-{step}.pt"
    torch.save({
        "epoch": epoch,
        "step": step,
        "model_state": model.state_dict(),
        "optimizer_state": optimizer.state_dict(),
    }, path)
    blob_name = f"{JOB_ID}/ckpt-{epoch}-{step}.pt"
    with open(path, "rb") as f:
        blob_client.get_blob_client(CONTAINER, blob_name).upload_blob(f, overwrite=True)
    print(f"Checkpoint saved: {blob_name}")
    os.remove(path)

def load_latest_checkpoint(model, optimizer):
    container = blob_client.get_container_client(CONTAINER)
    blobs = sorted(
        [b.name for b in container.list_blobs(name_starts_with=f"{JOB_ID}/")],
        reverse=True
    )
    if not blobs:
        return 0, 0  # no checkpoint, start from scratch
    latest = blobs[0]
    local = f"/tmp/latest_ckpt.pt"
    with open(local, "wb") as f:
        f.write(container.get_blob_client(latest).download_blob().readall())
    ckpt = torch.load(local, map_location="cpu")
    model.load_state_dict(ckpt["model_state"])
    optimizer.load_state_dict(ckpt["optimizer_state"])
    print(f"Resumed from checkpoint: {latest} (epoch {ckpt['epoch']}, step {ckpt['step']})")
    return ckpt["epoch"], ckpt["step"]

# Training loop
model = MyModel()
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters())
start_epoch, start_step = load_latest_checkpoint(model, optimizer)

for epoch in range(start_epoch, NUM_EPOCHS):
    for step, batch in enumerate(dataloader):
        if epoch == start_epoch and step < start_step:
            continue  # skip already-processed steps
        loss = model(batch)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()
        if step % 100 == 0:
            save_checkpoint(model, optimizer, epoch, step)
Cost Monitoring for Spot Workloads

Because spot pricing fluctuates, your cost per unit of work varies. Track actual spot spend separately from on-demand compute to understand savings achieved and identify if spot price spikes are reducing your cost advantage.

# Query actual spot VM spend (filter by priority tag or meter name)
az costmanagement query \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --type Usage \
  --timeframe Custom \
  --time-period from=2024-06-01 to=2024-06-30 \
  --dataset-granularity Monthly \
  --dataset-filter "{"and":[{"dimensions":{"name":"MeterName","operator":"In","values":["D4s v5 Spot"]}}]}" \
  --output table

# Tag all spot VMSS with environment=spot to filter in Cost Management
az vmss update \
  --resource-group <rg> \
  --name spot-worker-vmss \
  --set tags.priority=spot tags.team=data-platform

# Estimate savings vs PAYG for the same workload
def calculate_spot_savings(spot_cost: float, payg_rate: float, hours_run: float) -> dict:
    """
    spot_cost: actual amount paid for spot usage
    payg_rate: $/hr for the same SKU at PAYG rates
    hours_run: total VM-hours consumed
    """
    payg_equivalent = payg_rate * hours_run
    savings = payg_equivalent - spot_cost
    pct = (savings / payg_equivalent * 100) if payg_equivalent > 0 else 0
    return {
        "spot_cost": round(spot_cost, 2),
        "payg_equivalent": round(payg_equivalent, 2),
        "savings": round(savings, 2),
        "savings_pct": round(pct, 1),
    }

# Example: 1000 VM-hours of D4s_v5 at spot
result = calculate_spot_savings(
    spot_cost=120.00,   # actual billed
    payg_rate=0.192,    # D4s_v5 PAYG rate in eastus
    hours_run=1000,
)
print(f"Spot cost:       ${result['spot_cost']}")
print(f"PAYG equivalent: ${result['payg_equivalent']}")
print(f"Savings:         ${result['savings']} ({result['savings_pct']}%)")
Eviction Rate and Region Selection

Not all regions and SKUs have the same eviction frequency. High-demand regions (eastus, westeurope) generally have higher eviction rates for popular SKUs during peak hours. The Azure Portal shows eviction rate buckets (0-5%, 5-10%, >10%) when you select a VM size for spot.
Eviction Rate Bucket
Meaning
Strategy
Very low — capacity is plentiful
Good for longer-running jobs; safe to use without checkpointing
Moderate — occasional evictions expected
Use checkpointing every 10-15 minutes
High — frequent evictions likely
Short tasks only; or use Batch for automatic requeue

To find low-eviction-rate regions programmatically, query spot prices across multiple regions and correlate with historical eviction data. Azure does not expose raw eviction rates via API; the bucketed rate is available only in the Portal during VM creation.

# Compare spot prices across regions for the same SKU
for region in eastus westus2 northeurope eastasia australiaeast; do
  price=$(az rest \
    --method get \
    --url "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices?api-version=2023-01-01-preview&\$filter=serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and skuName eq 'D4s v5 Spot' and armRegionName eq '${region}'" \
    --query "Items[0].retailPrice" \
    --output tsv 2>/dev/null || echo "N/A")
  echo "${region}: \$${price}/hr"
done
Summary

Azure Spot VMs provide discounts of up to 90% on unused compute capacity, at the cost of 30-second eviction notices when capacity is needed or your max price is exceeded. Use Spot VMs inside VM Scale Sets for resilient batch processing, CI/CD agents, and ML training jobs; poll the IMDS Scheduled Events endpoint to handle evictions gracefully; and set max price to -1 to avoid price-spike evictions while still benefiting from spot discounts. Azure Batch is the simplest option for task-parallel workloads because it requeues interrupted tasks automatically.
